metadata.title: Identity Theft Prevention and Recovery
metadata.subtitle: Protect Your Information and Recover If Compromised

content.title: Identity Theft Prevention and Recovery
content.subtitle: Protect Your Information and Recover If Compromised

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Maria checked her credit card statement and noticed several large purchases she didn't make—electronics totaling nearly $3,000, shipped to an unfamiliar address across the country.

She didn't know how her information was stolen, and she felt paralyzed about what to do next.

What should Maria do immediately, and how could she have prevented this from happening?

objectives.description: Identify different tactics used by identity thieves and how they operate across digital and physical methods.
Evaluate strategies for protecting your personal information from both high-tech and low-tech theft.
Explain the step-by-step process for responding to identity theft, from discovery through recovery.
Differentiate between credit freezes and fraud alerts, knowing when to use each protection tool.

terms.definition: Fraudulent acquisition and use of a person's private identifying information, usually for financial gain.
terms.example: Someone uses your SSN and birthdate to open credit cards, take out loans, or file tax returns in your name.

terms.definition: A security measure that restricts access to your credit report, making it harder for thieves to open accounts in your name.
terms.example: With a freeze, even if a thief has all your info, they can't open new credit because lenders can't access your report.

terms.definition: A notice placed on your credit report that alerts creditors you may be a victim of fraud and they should verify identity before extending credit.
terms.example: A fraud alert means lenders must take extra steps to confirm it's really you before approving new accounts.

terms.definition: Illegal copying of information from the magnetic strip of a credit or ATM card using a hidden device.
terms.example: A thief installs a device on a gas pump card reader that captures card numbers from every customer.

leftPanel.title: Identity Theft Is an Epidemic
paragraphs: Identity theft affects millions of Americans annually, with losses reaching billions of dollars.
Beyond immediate financial impacts, victims face damaged credit scores and months of recovery work.
The FTC estimates victims spend 200+ hours over 6 months resolving identity theft—that's like a part-time job.

highlightBox.text: The average victim doesn't discover the theft for 3+ months—by then, significant damage has occurred.

stats.label: Identity theft reports to FTC annually

leftColumn.title: Digital Methods
items: Data breaches exposing millions of records

layoutData.title: Know the Red Flags—Early Detection Is Critical
paragraphs: The sooner you catch identity theft, the less damage it causes. Watch for these warning signs:
bulletPoints: Unexpected credit denial: You're denied for credit you should qualify for—someone may have damaged your score
Missing mail: Bills or statements stop arriving—a thief may have redirected your mail
Unknown accounts: Your credit report shows accounts you never opened
Collection calls: Collectors contact you about debts you don't recognize
IRS notices: The IRS says someone already filed using your SSN
Medical bills: You receive bills for medical services you never received

keyPoint.text: 📋 Check your credit reports regularly—you're entitled to one FREE report from each bureau annually at AnnualCreditReport.com

layoutData.title: Build Your Defense System
paragraphs: Protection requires addressing both digital and physical vulnerabilities.
bulletPoints: Secure your mail: Use a locking mailbox, opt for electronic statements, collect mail promptly
Shred everything: Destroy documents with personal info before discarding—cross-cut shredders are best
Protect cards physically: Check ATMs/gas pumps for skimmers, cover your PIN, use contactless when possible
Monitor regularly: Check bank statements weekly, review credit reports at least twice yearly
Use credit freezes: Freeze your credit with all three bureaus—it's FREE and stops most account fraud
Strong passwords: Unique passwords for each account, with two-factor authentication enabled

keyPoint.text: 🔐 A credit freeze is the SINGLE MOST EFFECTIVE protection—it stops 90% of new account identity theft.

leftColumn.title: Credit Freeze
items: BLOCKS all access to credit report
Must unfreeze when you want credit

rightColumn.title: Fraud Alert
items: Lasts 1 year (or 7 if ID theft victim)

content.headerTitle: If You Become a Victim: Recovery Steps

layoutData.title: Act Immediately—Time Is Critical
paragraphs: Follow these steps in order for the most effective recovery:
bulletPoints: Step 1: Call affected companies immediately to close or freeze compromised accounts
Step 2: Place a fraud alert with one credit bureau (they'll notify the others)
Step 3: File a report with the FTC at IdentityTheft.gov—get your recovery plan
Step 4: Change all passwords and PINs for affected and related accounts
Step 5: File a police report if needed (especially for tax fraud or criminal activity)
Step 6: Continue monitoring—thieves often try again once they have your info

keyPoint.text: 📝 IdentityTheft.gov creates a personalized recovery plan with pre-filled letters and step-by-step guidance.

content.headerTitle: Types of Identity Theft Beyond Credit Cards

leftPanel.title: Identity Theft Takes Many Forms
paragraphs: Tax Identity Theft: Someone files a tax return using your SSN to claim your refund before you can file.
Medical Identity Theft: Someone uses your insurance to get healthcare, leaving you with bills and corrupted medical records.
Child Identity Theft: Children's SSNs are stolen because they have clean credit histories.
Synthetic Identity Theft: Thieves combine real and fake info to create new identities.

infoCard.title: Why Check All Three Bureaus?
items: Not all lenders report to all bureaus
Fraud may appear on only one

scenario.name: The Gas Station Skimmer
paragraphs: After using his debit card at a gas pump, Jordan noticed fraudulent charges on his account days later.
The gas station later announced that thieves had installed hidden skimming devices on their card readers, capturing hundreds of customers' card information.

scenario.name: David's Discarded Documents
paragraphs: David threw away his old bank statements and pre-approved credit card offers without shredding them.
A month later, someone opened a credit card in his name using information from those documents retrieved from his trash.

content.question:
Maria (from The Challenge) discovered $3,000 in fraudulent charges on her credit card.

What are the FIRST THREE actions she should take, in order?

How would her response differ if she had discovered this on a debit card instead of a credit card?

Which identity theft protection measure do you think is most commonly overlooked by people your age?

Why might people neglect this precaution, and what could be the long-term consequences?

questions.question: What is the key difference between a credit freeze and a fraud alert? When would you use each?
Why would an identity thief file a tax return in someone else's name?
List the first THREE steps you should take if you discover you're an identity theft victim.
Why is it important to check your credit report from ALL THREE bureaus, not just one?

takeaways.title: Know the Tactics
takeaways.description: Thieves use both digital (phishing, data breaches) and physical (skimming, mail theft) methods—protect against both.

takeaways.title: Prevention Is Key
takeaways.description: Credit freezes, shredding documents, and monitoring accounts are your first line of defense.

takeaways.title: Recognize Warning Signs
takeaways.description: Unexpected denials, missing mail, and unknown accounts are red flags—catch theft early to limit damage.

takeaways.title: Know the Recovery Steps
takeaways.description: Act immediately: freeze accounts, place fraud alerts, report to FTC, change passwords, file police report if needed.

main.title: Identity Protection Audit
main.description: You've learned how identity theft happens—but how protected are YOU right now? Tomorrow you'll conduct a personal security audit and build your protection system.

steps: Audit your current protection measures (mail, passwords, card habits)
Identify your top three vulnerability points
Research how to place a credit freeze with all three bureaus
Create your personal identity theft response plan
Build a secure password system with a password manager

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
